texts.full_text: Balls featuring Honey Mama’s Nibs & Coffee bar. Unlike the eclipse, however, you can experience these whenever you like, so go on and whip up a batch to share today. Eclipse Balls by Kate Gavlick (follow her for more great recipes) 1 Honey Mama’s Nibs and Coffee bar ¼ cup coconut butter ½ cup pecans 1 heaped Tablespoon coconut oil 2 tablespoons cold brew coffee (we used Stumptown) ¼ cup unsweetened coconut flakes Combine all ingredients, except for coconut flakes, in a food processor and pulse until combined and sticky, but not overly broken down – just about 15-20 seconds. Scoop a heaped tablespoon of mixture into hands and roll into a ball. Dip into coconut flakes to make an eclipse pattern. Repeat for the remainder of the mixture. Store eclipse balls in the freezer for up to six months. Enjoy! Share this: Click to share onFacebook (Opens in new window) Click to share on Twitter (Opens in new window) Click to s

texts.full_text: se of a short-acting drug or 72-96 hours following methadone. Duration and intensity of withdrawal are related to clearance of the drug such that withdrawal is shorter (5-10 days) and more intense for opioids like morphine and less severe and more protracted with methadone. Prevention Opioid withdrawal syndrome should always be prevented. Patients treated with opioids for more than one to two weeks should be instructed to gradually reduce the opioid before discontinuing use. In general, dose reductions of about 20-25% every day or two will allow a tapering schedule to prevent signs and symptoms of withdrawal. An alternative recommendation is to give half the previous dose for the first 2 days and then reduce the dose by 25% every 2 days. When the dose reaches the equivalent of approximately 30 mg/day of PO morphine, this dose is given for 2 days, and then the drug is discontinued. It is important to continue to provide around-the-clock opioids to prevent withdrawal in the patient at end-of-life who is no longer able to communicate or take oral opioids. Treatment Clonidine 0.1-0.2mg PO Q 4-6 hours PRN or by transdermal patch (clonidine transdermal 0.1mg/24hour patch which provides 0.1mg a day for 7 days) can be used to treat autonomic hyperactivity symptoms (however, it will not relieve insomnia). The major drawback of clonidine therapy is the tendency to cause hypotension in some patients. Other agents used for control of withdrawal symptoms include: diphenoxylate/atropine, hydroxyzine, trazodone, and dicyclomine hydrochloride. For patients still in pain who have abruptly stopped their opioids (because they ran out, lost their prescription, or stopped because of side effects) reinstituting opioid therapy may be appropriate to treat both their withdrawal symptoms and ongoing pain. texts.full_text: a subset of normal and systemic sclerosis patient sera. To investigate whether agonist anti-platelet-derived growth factor receptor alpha (anti-PDGFRalpha) antibodies are present in the serum of patients with systemic sclerosis (SSc; scleroderma). Sera were obtained from healthy subjects and scleroderma patients. An electrochemiluminescence binding assay was performed for detection of serum autoantibodies to PDGFRalpha, PDGFRbeta, epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), and colony-stimulating factor receptor 1 (CSFR1). Serum immunoglobulin was purified by protein A/G chromatography. To assess Ig agonist activity, PDGFRalpha-expressing cells were incubated with pure Ig and the level of receptor phosphorylation determined in an enzyme-linked immunoassay, as well as by Western blotting. Ig agonist activity was also assessed in a mitogenic assay and by MAP kinase activation in a PDGFRalpha-expressing cell line. Sera from 34.3% of the healthy subjects and 32.7% of the SSc patients contained detectable autoantibodies to PDGFRalpha and PDGFRbeta, but not EGFR or CSFR1. This sounds ridiculous, but I would like an expert opinion. A: The CG range is defined by a combination of handling qualities and structural strength. Too far forward will give poor pitch down authority (because you're fighting against aerodynamic nose up forces) too aft gives high cRuise drag because tailplane has to work hard. For airliners the most critical requirement are stability at very low speeds i.e when on approach or during go around, with flaps and slats deployed. texts.full_text: CHICAGO -- Rookie Logan Schafer's three-run triple in the Brewers' four-run fourth inning turned out to be the offensive highlight of the game, ultimately providing the game-deciding runs as Milwaukee beat the Chicago Cubs 5-3 Saturday afternoon at Wrigley Field. The Brewers and Cubs both came into the day with identical 60-80 records, far out of the playoff picture in the National League with almost a month still left to play. Still, the managers of both teams said there are plenty of motivational factors to keep their guys grinding away -- even when the games they're playing don't really mean all that much in the NL Central standings. "Selfishly, they're still playing for numbers, they're still playing for contracts, they're still trying to stay in the big leagues (and) the young guys are trying to get in the big leagues," Brewers manager Ron Roenicke said. "I think when you play for the spoiler (role), we have a big opportunity to do that with all the teams in our division that we play -- so there's a lot of things to play for. Hopefully they're playing to win. That's what ultimately I hope they do, but there are so many other things to motivate them to play hard and to keep going and get after it." texts.full_text: eputy Chief Executive Officer in Charge of Operation at the National Health Insurance Authority, Mr. Nathaniel Otoo has promised there will be a sustenance of a more healthier Ghanaian citizens in decades to come thanks to the introduction of the Biometric Membership Solution (BMS) through the National Health Insurance Scheme. He reveals that the new biometric form of subscription has enabled the NHIA to register more new clients and currently renewing memberships of thousands more by the day. He said this in an exclusive interview with Moderngahan.com after a press conference in Accra. Mr. Otoo reveals that before the introduction of the biometric registration system, the NHIA could boast of 9.8 million subscription in 2013 with over 27 million hospital attendance annually. He argued that the introduction of the biometric ID card acquisition system will eventually cement the grand vision of the NHIA in offering affordable healthcare system in the country. Outlining the core basis for the exercise, Mr. Otoo enumerated that the profits are countless as it minimises the opportunities for abuse and fraud and promotes cost-containment. So far three regions have had their share of the instant biometric ID issuance being Greater Accra, Central, and Eastern regions respectively while preparations are underway to give the AshGold, a region renowned for its dense population, the same treatment. He revealed that the authority is working assiduously to roll out the same system to the remaining regions in the country. Mr. Otoo further explained that the new system is a game-changer in the health insurance industry in the country, as it is more efficient, secure, and user-friendly. He noted that the biometric technology captures the unique identity of each client, making it impossible for anyone to use another person's identity to access health care services. He also mentioned that the new system has the potential to reduce the number of uninsured Ghanaians, as it simplifies the registration process and makes it more accessible

texts.full_text: cool, but sunny weather of last week has given way to rain this morning. I don’t mind too much, because rainy days are perfect for snuggling up with my laptop and Bon Iver and catching up on my blogging. Also, we had boeuf bourguignon with braised spice cabbage for lunch, which is the perfect rainy fall day meal! Oh my tastebuds are happy. So, backing up to last Saturday. The best thing about having a rental car was the freedom of exploring rural Normandy (or Normandie) at my leisure (well, more or less, as much leisure as I could pack into 48 hours). Saturday, Sophie decided she wanted to have a down day, so I was on my own. I was a bit nervous about doing both the navigating and driving, but the great thing about country roads is you can pull off to the side and look at maps and directions when you need to. Also, cars can turn around, which is oh so handy! Saturday dawned cold clear morning, so a mist was just rising off the fields as I set off, making for some gorgeous, eerie type photos. By afternoon, it warmed to the point of being comfortable in long sleeves and sans jacket, with sun shining brightly in the sky. I drove to the town of Giverny, famous for being the home of Claude Monet, and spent some time exploring the charming streets and visiting the Monet Foundation, which features some of the artist's famous water lily paintings. The town's quaint atmosphere and the Foundation's beautiful gardens, which Monet himself designed, were the perfect antidote to the chill of the morning. The gardens were ablaze with the vibrant colors of the changing leaves, and the water lily pond, with its famous Japanese bridge, was a sight to behold. I It's understandable to feel concerned about safety while using shared pathways that require both pedestrians and cyclists to keep left (or right, depending on your location). The general rule for roads—walking facing traffic—is indeed based on visibility and reaction time in case of an emergency. However, the rules can be different or adapted when it comes to dedicated paths. Here are a few points to consider: 1. Shared Path Rules: Shared pathways often have specific guidelines designed with both users' safety in mind. Keeping left (or right) might mean that cyclists and pedestrians share space more efficiently while maintaining some level of predictability. 2. Visibility for Cyclists: For cyclists, having all traffic moving the same direction can improve their ability to see approaching obstacles or other path-users clearly without constantly checking both sides mirrors as they would on a road with mixed-directional flow. 3. texts.full_text: take input from the command line? My program allows the user to specify a file which is read as input, however this is optional. If the user does not specify a file, I'd like to read input in from the command line. I have this so far: main :: IO() main = do (opts, mbArgs) <- parseCmdLine input <- case mbArgs of Nothing -> getContents Just file -> readFile file This doesn't seem to be working. When a user doesn't stipulate a file, they are able to enter input, but there seems to be no way of terminating so that the program can then work on that input. I thought that you had to press Ctrl+D, but that doesn't do anything. Thanks for any help. A: In a typical Unix-like terminal (such as Cygwin, at least in Cygwin or Git Bash), the standard way of sending input and signaling end-of-file is by pressing Ctrl+D together with your text being entered into the command line interface. This action sends EOF to both stdin and stdout streams which can be caught using appropriate functions like getContents for reading from a file descriptor, as you are doing in this case. However, if it's not working because of some specific reason or another terminal environment (like Windows cmd), then the solution might vary depending on your exact setup: - You could use Ctrl+Z and SIGINT to simulate end-of-file for a command line program under windows as per this answer. This is similar in spirit but different from what you'd do with Unix/Linux terminal where it would be just Ctrl + D. Here’s how your code should look like: ```haskell main :: IO() main = do (opts, mbArgs) <- parseCmdLine input <- case mbArgs of Nothing -> getContents Just file -> readFile file -- Here you can add logic to process the input. texts.full_text: is study was to determine the prevalence of High Risk HPV and cytological abnormalities at first follow-up visit post treatment. The feasibility, safety and cost benefit of omitting routine colposcopy as a first line investigation were evaluated. A total of 100 women who had undergone treatment for HSIL were included in the study. The median age of the women was 32 years (range 20-57 years). The treatments received included large loop excision of the transformation zone (LLETZ), cold coagulation, laser ablation, and hysterectomy. At the first follow-up visit, high-risk HPV testing was performed using the cobas HPV test (Roche Molecular Systems, Pleasanton, CA, USA), which detects 14 high-risk HPV types. Cytological abnormalities were assessed using the Bethesda 2001 classification system. The results showed that 22% (22/100) of the women tested positive for high-risk HPV, and 16% (16/100) had cytological abnormalities. Of the 16 women with cytological abnormalities, 10 (62.5%) were high-risk HPV positive. The study also evaluated the feasibility, safety, and cost-benefit of omitting routine colposcopy as the first-line investigation. It was found that high-risk HPV testing alone was feasible and safe, with no adverse events reported. Additionally, omitting colposcopy resulted in cost savings of £110 per patient. In conclusion, the prevalence of high-risk HPV and cytological abnormalities at the first follow-up visit post-treatment for HSIL was 22% and 16%, respectively. High-risk HPV testing alone was shown to be a feasible, safe, and cost-effective alternative to routine

texts.full_text: grisly details that usually pepper Joe’s murder investigations and shifting towards a psychological thriller. Tag Man allows ArcherMayor (who often fuses both surnames together) an opportunity for character exploration, particularly of his titular detective as he grapples with trauma in ways not typically seen through such lensed plotlines. The mystery at the heart – who is this tagger? Why does someone seemingly harmless choose to commit these odd intrusions into private homes while leaving no trail behind him - creates a subtle yet gripping atmosphere. It contrasts sharply against Joe's personal turmoil, thereby enhancing both elements of his internal and external journey through its stark differences. In "Tag Man", Archer Mayor crafts an intriguing narrative that balances between the traditional detective novel structure he is known for with introspective themes seldom explored in genre fiction such as this – making it a refreshing addition to what many might see solely within standard police procedural bounds. It offers readers not just another whodunit but also questions around identity, recovery from loss and mental resilience against insurmountable odds. Overall Rating: 4 out of 5 stars "Tag Man", while slightly off the beaten path for Joe Gunther enthusiasts looking primarily to unravel homicides or delve into forensic details quickly becomes compelling due its psychological dept

Mahony applauds during the December installation of… (Genaro Molina, Los Angeles…) In a move unprecedented in the American Catholic Church, Los Angeles Archbishop Jose Gomez announced Thursday that he had relieved his predecessor, Cardinal Roger M. Mahony, of all public duties over his mishandling of clergy sex abuse of children decades ago. Gomez also said that Auxiliary Bishop Thomas J. Curry, who worked with Mahony to conceal abusers from police in the 1980s, had resigned his post as a regional bishop in Santa Barbara. The announcement came as the church posted on its website tens of thousands of pages of previously secret personnel files for 122 priests accused of molesting children. "I find these files to be brutal and painful reading.
